questions.promptTex: \text{At } \theta = 90¬į, \text{ the point on the unit circle is:}
hints: $\theta = 90¬į$ points straight up (positive y-direction).
questions.explanation: At $90¬į$, the radius points along the positive y-axis, hitting the circle at $(0, 1)$. So $\cos 90¬į = 0$ and $\sin 90¬į = 1$.
steps: Sketch the unit circle. Standard position means the angle starts on the positive x-axis.
Rotate counterclockwise by $90¬į$ ‚ÄĒ that's a quarter turn. The terminal side now points straight up along the positive y-axis.
It hits the unit circle at the point $(0, 1)$.

hints: $180¬į$ points along the negative x-axis.
The point on the circle is $(-1, 0)$.
questions.explanation: At $180¬į$, the unit-circle point is $(-1, 0)$. The x-coordinate is $\cos 180¬į = -1$.
steps: $180¬į$ is half a revolution counterclockwise from the positive x-axis.
The terminal side ends along the **negative** x-axis.
It hits the unit circle at $(-1, 0)$.

hints: $\cos < 0$ means $x < 0$ ‚ÄĒ left half of the plane.
$\sin > 0$ means $y > 0$ ‚ÄĒ upper half. Where do those overlap?
questions.explanation: $\cos < 0$ puts us on the left half ($x < 0$); $\sin > 0$ puts us on the upper half ($y > 0$). That's Quadrant II ‚ÄĒ angles between $90¬į$ and $180¬į$.
steps: On the unit circle, $\cos\theta = x$ and $\sin\theta = y$.
$\cos < 0 \Rightarrow x < 0$ ‚Üí the **left** half of the plane (Q II or Q III).
$\sin > 0 \Rightarrow y > 0$ ‚Üí the **upper** half (Q I or Q II).

hints: This is the Pythagorean identity.
$\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ for *every* angle.
questions.explanation: By the Pythagorean identity, $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ always. The angle doesn't matter ‚ÄĒ every point on the unit circle satisfies $x^2 + y^2 = 1$.
steps: Recognize the form: $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta$. This is the **Pythagorean identity**.

hints: $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$.
At $90¬į$, $\cos 90¬į = 0$. What happens when you divide by zero?
questions.explanation: $\tan 90¬į = \dfrac{\sin 90¬į}{\cos 90¬į} = \dfrac{1}{0}$. Division by zero is undefined, so $\tan 90¬į$ is **undefined**.
steps: Division by zero is **undefined**, so $\tan 90¬į$ is undefined.

questions.promptTex: \text{In which quadrant does the terminal side of } 215¬į \text{ lie?}
hints: Where does $215¬į$ fall?
questions.explanation: $180¬į < 215¬į < 270¬į$, so the terminal side is in **Quadrant III**.
steps: The four quadrants by angle range: Q I ($0$‚Äď$90¬į$), Q II ($90$‚Äď$180¬į$), Q III ($180$‚Äď$270¬į$), Q IV ($270$‚Äď$360¬į$).
So the terminal side lies in **Quadrant III**.

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the angle in } [0¬į, 360¬į) \text{ that is coterminal with } 460¬į.
hints: Coterminal angles differ by full rotations of $360¬į$.
Subtract $360¬į$ until the result is in $[0¬į, 360¬į)$.
steps: Two angles are **coterminal** if they differ by a whole number of full revolutions ($\pm 360¬į$).
$460¬į$ is more than one full rotation. Subtract $360¬į$: $$460¬į - 360¬į = 100¬į$$

questions.promptTex: \text{Find the smallest positive angle coterminal with } -120¬į.
hints: Add $360¬į$ until the result is positive.
steps: Negative angles rotate clockwise. To find a coterminal positive angle, add $360¬į$.
$240¬į$ is the smallest positive angle coterminal with $-120¬į$.

hints: $150¬į$ is in Quadrant II.
Reference angle in Q II: $\alpha = 180¬į - \theta$.
steps: Identify the quadrant of $150¬į$: $90¬į < 150¬į < 180¬į$, so **Q II**.

hints: $300¬į$ is in Quadrant IV.
Reference angle in Q IV: $\alpha = 360¬į - \theta$.

questions.promptTex: \text{In Quadrant III, which trig functions are positive?}
hints: ASTC mnemonic: All Students Take Calculus, going counterclockwise from Q I.
Q I ‚Üí All. Q II ‚Üí Sin. Q III ‚Üí Tan. Q IV ‚Üí Cos.
questions.explanation: ASTC: in Quadrant III, only **T**angent is positive (sin and cos are both negative, but tan = sin/cos = (-)/(-) > 0).
steps: Recall the **ASTC** mnemonic going counterclockwise from Q I: All‚ÄďSin‚ÄďTan‚ÄďCos.
Q III is the third quadrant (counting counterclockwise), which corresponds to **T**: only **tangent** is positive.
Sanity check: in Q III, $x < 0$ and $y < 0$, so $\sin = y < 0$ and $\cos = x < 0$. But $\tan = y/x =$ negative/negative $> 0$. ‚úď

questions.promptTex: \text{If } \theta \text{ is in Quadrant II, what is the sign of } \cos\theta?
hints: In Q II, only sine is positive.
$\cos\theta = x$, and in Q II $x < 0$.
steps: In Q II, points have $x < 0$ and $y > 0$.
Since $\cos\theta = x$, $\cos\theta$ is **negative** in Q II.
ASTC confirms: in Q II only **S**ine is positive ‚ÄĒ cos and tan are both negative.

hints: $210¬į$ is in Q III. Reference angle is $210¬į - 180¬į = 30¬į$.
$\cos 30¬į = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. In Q III, cosine is negative.
steps: **Step 2.** Find the reference angle (Q III rule): $$\alpha = 210¬į - 180¬į = 30¬į$$
**Step 3.** Look up $\cos 30¬į$ from the special-angle table: $\cos 30¬į = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.
**Step 4.** Apply the sign from ASTC. In Q III, cosine is **negative** (only T is positive).

hints: $315¬į$ is in Q IV. Reference angle is $360¬į - 315¬į = 45¬į$.
$\sin 45¬į = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$. In Q IV, sine is negative.
steps: **Step 4.** ASTC: in Q IV, only cosine is positive ‚ÄĒ so sine is **negative**.

hints: $225¬į$ is in Q III. Reference angle is $225¬į - 180¬į = 45¬į$.
$\tan 45¬į = 1$. In Q III, tangent is positive (ASTC).
steps: **Step 4.** ASTC: in Q III, **T**angent is **positive**. (Both sin and cos are negative, so tan = neg/neg = positive.)

hints: Convert to a quadrant: $\pi < \tfrac{4\pi}{3} < \tfrac{3\pi}{2}$, so Q III.
Reference angle: $\tfrac{4\pi}{3} - \pi = \tfrac{\pi}{3}$. In Q III, sine is negative.
questions.explanation: $\sin\tfrac{\pi}{3} = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, and Q III sine is negative, so $\sin\tfrac{4\pi}{3} = -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.
steps: **Step 1.** Locate the quadrant. $\pi \approx 3.14$ and $\tfrac{3\pi}{2} \approx 4.71$, so $\tfrac{4\pi}{3} \approx 4.19$ falls between them ‚Üí **Q III**.
**Step 4.** ASTC: in Q III, sine is **negative**.

hints: $\tfrac{5\pi}{6}$ is in Q II. Reference angle: $\pi - \tfrac{5\pi}{6} = \tfrac{\pi}{6}$.
$\cos\tfrac{\pi}{6} = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. In Q II, cosine is negative.
steps: **Step 4.** ASTC: in Q II only sine is positive ‚ÄĒ cosine is **negative**.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the angle (in radians) in } [0, 2\pi) \text{ coterminal with } \dfrac{9\pi}{4}?
hints: Coterminal angles differ by $2\pi$.
$\tfrac{9\pi}{4} - 2\pi = \tfrac{9\pi}{4} - \tfrac{8\pi}{4} = ?$
steps: Coterminal angles in radians differ by $2\pi$.
Subtract $2\pi$ from $\dfrac{9\pi}{4}$. First express $2\pi$ with denominator $4$: $$2\pi = \dfrac{8\pi}{4}$$
